content:
3 min read
Just now

--

There was a time when I thought code reviews had one simple purpose.
Find mistakes.
If my pull request was approved with only a couple of comments, I considered it a good day. If it was approved without any comments, even better.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Then one review completely changed the way I looked at them.
It wasn’t a complicated feature.
Just another ticket from the sprint board.
I spent the day building it, tested everything, cleaned up the code, and opened a pull request feeling confident.
The feature worked exactly as expected.
I thought the difficult part was over.
About an hour later, the review came back.
I expected comments about my implementation.
Maybe a better function name.
Maybe an edge case I had missed.
Instead, the first comment said, “What happens if this requirement changes in the future?”
I paused.
That wasn’t the question I expected.
The requirement hadn’t changed.
The ticket was about today’s problem, and my solution solved it.
Why were we talking about the future?
I replied by explaining why my implementation met every requirement.
A few minutes later, another comment appeared.
“I know it solves today’s problem. I’m asking whether this code still makes sense if the requirement changes next month.”
At the time, I didn’t understand why that mattered.
We discussed a few changes, I updated the implementation, and the pull request was approved.
I moved on.
Months later, I found myself working on features that had grown far beyond their original requirements.
Code that once looked perfectly reasonable had become difficult to extend.
Not because it was badly written.
Because it had only been designed for the problem that existed on the day it was created.
That’s when that review came back to me.
For the first time, I understood the question.
The reviewer wasn’t asking me to predict the future.
They were asking whether I had left enough room for change.
One day, while reviewing someone else’s pull request, I caught myself typing the exact same comment.
“What happens if this requirement changes in the future?”
I stopped for a moment.
Then I smiled.
Months earlier, that question had confused me.
Now it felt completely natural.
That review wasn’t about my coding ability.
It wasn’t about JavaScript, React, or writing cleaner code.
It was about how I was thinking.
I was focused on finishing the ticket.
My reviewer was thinking about what would happen after the ticket was closed.
These days, I still care about writing clean code.
I still rename variables and simplify functions.
But before opening a pull request, I ask myself one question I never used to ask.
If someone has to change this six months from now, will they understand why I built it this way?
I don’t always have the perfect answer.
But asking that question has changed the way I build software.
Looking back, I thought code reviews existed to improve code.
Now I think they exist to improve thinking.
And that’s a lesson I didn’t understand until years later.
